briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome to the climate briefing for May 14, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Climate Threats and the Approaching Catastrophic Reality
sections.content: The direct impacts of climate change are intensifying globally, particularly in the United States, where 'water inflation' is escalating to more than double the overall inflation rate [1]. Experts are warning that the upcoming 2026 FIFA World Cup will inevitably face disruptions to its schedule due to extreme heat and humidity [8, 110, 117], illustrating how climate change threatens even international sporting events. Scientists have discovered the Antarctic Ocean 'sweating' and warn that all hurricanes are becoming wetter, exacerbating flood risks [101, 112]. Furthermore, crisis signals across ecosystems are becoming clear, with tens of thousands of plant species facing extinction and emperor penguins also confronting extinction due to melting sea ice [111, 115]. These climate changes disproportionately affect marginalized communities, raising issues of environmental justice [120], and societal anxiety is growing, evidenced by the publication of books depicting a future of war in a world ravaged by climate change [100].

sections.title: ⚠️ Domestic Climate Vulnerability: Flash Droughts and Emergency Response
sections.content: Domestically, 'flash droughts' are becoming more frequent [13, 19], and the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) is warning of the possibility of a drought recurrence after 108 years, implementing an integrated meteorological drought information service for rapid detection [11, 16, 18, 21]. This clearly demonstrates the nation's vulnerability to unpredictable changes in climate patterns. Furthermore, it has been confirmed that climate change poses a direct threat to public health and safety, with a pathogenic Vibrio infection alert issued in the Jeju region due to climate change and rising sea temperatures [9]. In this context, issues such as allegations of inadequate pollutant reduction facilities at asphalt concrete plants for carbon neutrality [34], and the National Human Rights Commission's recommendation that carbon neutrality policies should not marginalize vulnerable populations [37], suggest side effects and the need for improvement in the process of responding to the climate crisis.

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Cooperation and Policy Promotion Towards a Carbon-Neutral Society
sections.content: Various domestic institutions are strengthening cooperation to respond to the climate crisis and achieve carbon neutrality. The Incheon Institute and the Korea Climate Change Research Institute signed an agreement for joint research on climate response and carbon neutrality [2, 3, 5, 6, 7], and the Climate Change Center and the Rights of Nature also joined forces in responding to the climate crisis through a business agreement [4]. Local governments are also showing active engagement: Geumsan County is responding to climate change by supporting medicinal herb farms [10], Gunwi County is leading efforts to combat heatwaves using cooling fog [12, 14, 20], and Hanam City is contributing to carbon neutrality by inspecting businesses that generate large amounts of food waste [25, 61]. The Korea Forest Service launched the Landslide Prevention Support Headquarters to proactively respond to the climate crisis [15], and Representative Park Sang-woong is working to establish institutional foundations, including proposing an amendment to the Carbon Neutrality Framework Act to strengthen climate safety nets for agriculture, fisheries, and businesses [28, 35, 44].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition: Public Leadership and Technological Innovation
sections.content: South Korea is accelerating its transition to renewable energy, striving to achieve its carbon neutrality goals. Public power generation companies such as Korea Midland Power, Korea Western Power, and Korea Southern Power are collaborating with the Korea Industrial Complex Corporation and Shinhan Asset Management to promote public-led industrial complex solar power projects, preparing for the RE100 era [22, 46, 71, 74, 76, 79, 81, 84, 89, 90, 91, 98]. Notably, the expansion of solar projects utilizing even abandoned highway idle land [88] and the analysis of potential income increase for farmers through agri-voltaics [99] are excellent examples of discovering renewable energy potential. Korea Water Resources Corporation (K-water) is accelerating its renewable energy transition using 'water' and seeking solutions for energy security [92], while Korean Register and Kumyang Shipping are pioneering carbon-neutral shipping routes with hybrid vessels [57]. These efforts demonstrate ongoing transition through technological innovation across various sectors. Additionally, Hana Bank is strengthening its carbon neutrality response by acquiring international standard certification for its energy management system [40, 54, 63, 93, 96].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Action and Ecosystem Changes
sections.content: The international community stands at a critical juncture for achieving climate justice. Next week in New York, the UN General Assembly is set to vote on a resolution supporting the International Court of Justice's (ICJ) groundbreaking advisory opinion on climate justice [121], which will be a significant step towards strengthening legal and political accountability for climate change. In the Himalayan region, climate change and tourism are altering agricultural practices [109], and the case of a neighborhood in Medellín, Colombia, transforming the city's entire approach to climate change planning [114] demonstrates how innovative local climate action can drive global change. Businesses are also exploring ways to address climate change beyond their operations [118], participating in international climate action.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severe global and domestic threats posed by climate change, while also demonstrating the simultaneous efforts in diverse domestic and international cooperation and renewable energy transition to overcome these challenges. This is a critical juncture that demands sustained attention and active participation. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: UN members prepare for pivotal vote on landmark ICJ climate justice ruling
articles.description: If the resolution is passed, governments will have legal responsibility to cut greenhouse gas emissions. The UN’s willingness to tackle the climate crisis in a fair and legal way will be tested next week during a critical vote of the UN General Assembly in New York. Every member state is being asked to back a series of landmark findings on climate justice from the International Court of Justice (ICJ) as part of a new political resolution. If passed, it will mean governments recognize they have a legal responsibility.

summary.phenomenon: Next week, at the UN General Assembly in New York, all member states are facing a critical vote on a new political resolution supporting the International Court of Justice (ICJ)'s landmark recommendations on climate justice.
summary.outlook: If this resolution passes, governments will bear legal responsibility for reducing greenhouse gas emissions, signifying their acknowledgment of this legal obligation.
